iteration_comparison.note: Iteration 2 assertions were strictly more demanding (16+17+7 vs 10+10+4 in iter-1). One new failure surfaced: eval-0 with_skill placed the grep output ('4 callers in src/payments/') under Verified instead of Observed. Per the skill's definitions, grep output is Observed (command result), not Verified (direct file read). This is a real bug in the skill's classification guidance that is worth addressing in a future iteration.

analyst_notes: The iteration-2 delta widened to +37.5pp (60.0% → 97.5%) because the expanded assertion set tests the skill's specific structural commitments (3-level taxonomy, per-item Verify actions, hedge-word demotion, agreement-scope disambiguation) — none of which the baseline reproduces.
The hedge-word demotion refinement (added in iter-2 SKILL.md) worked: eval-0 with_skill now explicitly called out 'usually' as the signal and placed the claim under Pattern-based.

The agreement-scope refinement worked strongly in eval-1: with_skill explicitly noted the user's 'sounds reasonable' only endorsed Postgres compatibility, not the full schema — and placed the schema claim under Hypothesis with a Verify action to re-confirm.

Single remaining failure (eval-0 with_skill): 'grep output under Verified' shows the skill's Verified/Observed distinction is still a little fuzzy — grep is technically a command whose output is being read. A tighter definition in the skill would fix this.

Baseline's notable failure pattern in eval-1: it conflated 'the assistant proposed X' with facts about the system by adding a 'From the assistant' sub-list inside the Facts section — this is the exact epistemic slippage the skill prevents by forcing proposal claims into Inference/Hypothesis.
with_skill remains faster on eval-2 (18.2s vs 38.7s) because the 'ask instead of audit' path is much shorter than writing a full audit.
